howItWorks.title: How CauseFlow investigates your problems
howItWorks.subtitle: 6 specialized AI agents investigate in parallel — from alert to root cause in ~3 minutes. End-to-end resolution including human approval: ~35 minutes vs. 2–4 hours manual.

receive.title: Problem received — 4:07 AM
receive.description: Checkout service returning 500 errors. CauseFlow classifies severity as Critical and begins investigating immediately — no manual triage needed.

investigate.title: 6 agents investigate in parallel
investigate.description: Log Analyst reads 847 error log lines. Metrics Analyst detects a latency spike at 4:05 AM. Change Detector finds 3 recent commits — one deployed at 3:58 AM. Infrastructure Inspector checks container restarts. Code Analyzer reads the affected service. Database Analyst queries connection pool state. All in parallel.

identify.title: Root cause identified — confidence 91%
identify.description: Four independent signals converge: deployment timestamp matches error spike, connection pool config change, error pattern in logs, metrics correlation. Confidence 91% — multiple sources agree.

recommend.title: Exact fix proposed — human approves
recommend.description: CauseFlow proposes: "Revert config max_connections from 50 to 200. This will restart 3 service tasks." You see the exact change, affected services, and estimated impact. Nothing executes without your approval.

learn.title: Pattern learned — next time: under 2 minutes
learn.description: The pattern is added to the Knowledge Base with a confidence score. Second occurrence: pattern matched in seconds, same fix proposed immediately. MTTR drops from ~35 min to under 2 minutes.

audit.title: Tamper-evident audit trail
audit.description: Every agent action is recorded: which sources were accessed, what data was read, and which decisions were made. Full accountability for every investigation.

crossTool.title: One Pipeline. Two Problem Sources.
crossTool.description: SRE alert at 4:07 AM: checkout returning 500 errors. 6 agents investigate — deployment correlation found, root cause identified in 6 minutes. Customer report at 9 AM: "my exports aren't working." The same pipeline investigates — same agents, same depth. Root cause identified. Customer-facing explanation generated alongside the technical fix. Both investigated by the same agent. No manual escalation. No handoff.
crossTool.vizFixDescription: Deployment v2.4.1 introduced a race condition in the data sync service, causing intermittent record deletion.

whyNow.title: Why Now?
whyNow.subtitle: Two converging forces are making automated incident investigation essential.

card1.title: AI-Generated Code Creates More Incidents
card1.body: 41% of all code is now AI-generated. Incidents per PR are up 23.5% year-over-year. More code shipped faster means more production problems to investigate.

card2.title: Engineers Spend More Time on Repetitive Ops, Not Less
card2.body: Despite billions invested in DevOps tooling, time spent on repetitive operational tasks rose from 25% to 30% of engineering time in 2025 — the first increase in five years. Current tools optimize workflows, but nobody automates the investigation itself.

whyDifferent.title: Why CauseFlow Exists
whyDifferent.subtitle: Current incident tools optimize the wrong part of the problem. Here's what they miss — and what we built to fix it.

builtByEM.title: Tools Optimize the Wrong 20%
builtByEM.description: Most incident resolution time is technical investigation — not coordination. Current tools automate coordination. Nobody automates the investigation itself. CauseFlow does.

minutesNotHours.title: Recurring Issues Resolve in Under 2 Minutes
minutesNotHours.description: First investigation: full multi-agent analysis, ~35 minutes end-to-end. Second occurrence: pattern matched in seconds, same fix proposed immediately — under 2 minutes. Your institutional knowledge stops living in engineers' heads.
minutesNotHours.source: The average incident investigation takes 2–4 hours across fragmented tools.

crossTool.title: Nobody Bridges SRE and Customer Support
crossTool.description: When a customer reports "exports aren't working," CauseFlow investigates it the same way it would a monitoring alert — same 6 agents, same depth. Root cause identified. Customer-facing explanation generated alongside the technical fix. No escalation to engineering. No manual handoff.

gracefulDegradation.title: Works With Whatever You Have Connected
gracefulDegradation.description: No GitHub? Code analysis is skipped — not broken. No cloud monitoring? Infrastructure agents sit out. Connect one tool today, add the rest over time. CauseFlow never fails because of a missing integration.

builtForStartups.title: Your Reputation Grows With Your Response Time
builtForStartups.description: Slow incident response means lost trust and reputation damage. CauseFlow gives teams of 2–50 engineers enterprise-grade investigation without enterprise pricing.

platformCompanies.title: Platform Companies Don't Compete at the Application Layer
platformCompanies.description: AWS didn't build Datadog. Google didn't build PagerDuty. AI platform companies provide the intelligence layer — we build the solution.

heroV2.lead: CauseFlow cross-references Slack, GitHub, CloudWatch, Datadog and Jira to surface production-grade root cause in minutes — with human-in-the-loop approval by default.
heroV2.trustText: Read-only by default — your data never trains a shared model.

narrative.lead: Read-only connectors, parallel AI agents per data source, and auditable debate between hypotheses — every conclusion links back to the evidence that produced it.
narrative.f1Desc: OAuth-scoped integrations with Slack, GitHub, Jira, Datadog, Sentry and CloudWatch. No agents on your boxes, no write permissions.
narrative.f2Desc: Six specialised agents run in parallel — one per data class. Findings are cross-referenced and ranked by confidence, not by who spoke loudest.
narrative.f3Desc: Every hypothesis cites its evidence. Engineers review, approve or reject — the full debate is stored as an immutable audit trail.

securityFirst.lead: Read-only by default. Your data never trains a shared model. SOC 2 Type II on the roadmap, LGPD and GDPR compliant today.
securityFirst.p1Desc: Zero write permissions unless you explicitly grant them. Every fix lands behind a human-approved PR.
securityFirst.p2Desc: Per-tenant encryption keys and strict data boundaries. Your investigation data stays in your tenant.
securityFirst.p3Desc: We never train foundation models on your incident data. Contractually, by design, by default.
securityFirst.p4Desc: Every prompt, retrieval and approval is captured to an append-only log for compliance review.

cards.title: Pricing page showing stale prices after CMS update
cards.finding: CMS invalidation webhook failing with 403 for 12min. CDN serving /pricing stale with 15min TTL. Manual purge + webhook secret rotation.

cards.title: Broken images on comparison page /from-momentic
cards.finding: S3 bucket comparison-assets with CORS policy changed in previous deploy. 14 images returning 403 for causeflow.ai origin only. Policy rollback applied in 1 command.

cards.title: API intermittently returning 500 errors on /payments
cards.finding: Connection pool exhaustion after deploy a3f2c1 that removed pool.release() in error branch. 8% of requests stalling until timeout. Fix PR generated with regression test.

cards.title: Checkout latency rising gradually during peak hours
cards.finding: N+1 on Order.items after serializer change. P95 rose from 180ms to 2.4s with 50+ cart items. Eager-loading proposed with 2-line preload.

alerts.lead: CauseFlow classifies every event. High or critical automatically opens an investigation — low goes to the human review feed.

cards.title: 🔍 INV-2847 · root cause identified
cards.body: Stale CDN cache — CMS invalidation webhook failing with 403. Proposed fix: manual purge + secret rotation. 94% confidence.

cards.title: INV-2844 · broken images
cards.body: CORS policy reverted. 14 assets serving normally. Incident closed automatically — audit trail at /incidents/2844.

cards.title: fix(payments): restore pool.release() in error branch

reasoning.headline: More than a detective — a shared brain for your company
reasoning.lead: CauseFlow doesn't just plug into your tools and uncover the root cause. It becomes a knowledge bank about your company and products — learning from every incident and helping you make better decisions.

paranoid.lead: Read-only by default. The agent works alongside you, asks for clarification mid-investigation, and never writes without your sign-off.

cards.title: No writing by default

q1.question: What is CauseFlow AI?
q1.answer: CauseFlow is an AI-powered incident investigation platform that deploys 6 specialized agents in parallel to find root causes in minutes instead of hours.

q2.question: How long does an investigation take?
q2.answer: CauseFlow identifies root cause in about 3 minutes. End-to-end resolution including human approval typically takes around 35 minutes versus 2-4 hours manual.

q3.question: Do fixes run without human approval?
q3.answer: No. CauseFlow proposes exact fixes but nothing executes without your explicit approval. If no decision is made within 30 minutes, the action is automatically cancelled.

hero.title:
How CauseFlow Solves
Incident Investigation
hero.subtitle: Stop spending 2–4 hours per incident switching tools. CauseFlow connects to your monitoring, code, and infrastructure tools, deploys 6 specialized agents in parallel, and delivers root cause with fix recommendations in ~3 minutes.
hero.metaDescription: CauseFlow deploys 6 specialized AI agents across your monitoring, code, and infrastructure tools to deliver root cause and fix recommendations in ~3 minutes.

phase1.title: Phase 1 —Assisted Investigation + Remediation

step1.title: Receives the problem
step1.description: Via web interface or API. Describe the problem in natural language — 'the checkout page is returning 500 errors' or 'a customer says their data was deleted' — and the agent starts investigating immediately. Both infrastructure alerts and customer-reported issues.

step2.title: Multiple specialized agents investigate in parallel
step2.description: Log Analyst — reads error logs, finds patterns and exceptions. Metrics Analyst — analyzes CPU, memory, latency, error rate. Infrastructure Inspector — checks service and container state, recent restarts. Change Detector — finds recent deployments, config changes, code pushes. Code Analyzer — reads relevant code via your connected repository. Database Analyst — queries database state and performance. Each agent gets temporary, read-only credentials scoped to exactly its data source — valid for 15 minutes. If you haven't connected an integration, that agent sits out gracefully.

step3.title: Analyzes and correlates across all sources
step3.description: Cross-references findings from all active agents. Generates hypotheses, tests each against available evidence, and assigns a confidence score (0-100%) reflecting how many independent data sources corroborate the finding. High confidence: multiple signals agree. Lower confidence: contradicting signals — CauseFlow flags the uncertainty.

step4.title: Delivers complete report
step4.description: Probable root cause + confidence score + chronological event timeline + specific fix recommendations + customer impact summary (if applicable). Entire investigation takes ~3 minutes.

phase1.remediation: CauseFlow proposes the exact fix: "Revert config max_connections from 50 to 200. This will restart 3 service tasks." You see the proposed change, the affected services, and the estimated impact. Tap Approve — and the fix executes. Nothing runs without your explicit approval. Timeout: if no decision in 30 minutes, the action is automatically cancelled.

phase2.title: Phase 2 —Intelligent Knowledge Base
phase2.headline: Every Investigation Makes CauseFlow Smarter
phase2.description: After each investigation, CauseFlow extracts the pattern — root cause signature, fix, confidence — and adds it to the Knowledge Base. Status progresses: Learning → Stable → Runbook Candidate.

firstOccurrence.label: First occurrence
firstOccurrence.detail: Full investigation by 6 specialized agents. Root cause identified in ~3 minutes. Human approves fix. Pattern extracted and added to the Knowledge Base.

secondOccurrence.label: Second occurrence
secondOccurrence.detail: Pattern matched in seconds. Same fix proposed immediately. Human approves. No full investigation needed. MTTR: 1m 47s.

kbEntry.label: Knowledge Base entry
kbEntry.signature: Root cause signature: max_connections config < 100 under sustained load
kbEntry.fix: Fix template: Revert max_connections to baseline + alert rule added

phase2.runbookNote: After multiple recurrences, CauseFlow flags this as a Runbook Candidate — your L1 support team can resolve it directly, without involving engineers.

phase3.title: Phase 3 —Autonomous Remediation
phase3.headline: From Reactive to Preventive
phase3.description: Using accumulated investigation data and production patterns, CauseFlow will proactively identify conditions likely to cause incidents before they impact customers — shifting your team from reactive firefighting to predictive prevention. Combined with autonomous remediation (deploy reverts, config adjustments, auto-scaling), always with human-in-the-loop for destructive actions. The goal: prevent incidents before your customers even notice.

auditTrail.title: See exactly what the agent did
auditTrail.subtitle: Total transparency. Every agent action is recorded in an immutable log visible to you.

architecture.title: Technical Architecture
architecture.connectivity: Connectivity layer: standard integration protocol servers (10,000+ available in the ecosystem)
architecture.core: Proprietary core: Planning engine, hypothesis generation, learning and Knowledge Base
architecture.llm: LLM Gateway: Uses lightweight models for log reading and data extraction. Reserves higher-capability models for final synthesis and root cause reasoning. This keeps investigations fast without sacrificing accuracy on the decisions that matter.
architecture.security: Security: AWS Bedrock (ISO/IEC 42001), KMS per-tenant, PII detection engine

q1.question: How does CauseFlow investigate incidents?
q1.answer: CauseFlow deploys 6 specialized AI agents in parallel across your monitoring, code, and infrastructure tools, then correlates findings to deliver root cause with confidence scores in about 3 minutes.

q2.question: What integrations does CauseFlow support?
q2.answer: CauseFlow connects to monitoring tools like Datadog and PagerDuty, code repositories like GitHub, ticketing systems like Jira, and customer support platforms. Each integration maps to a specialized agent.

q3.question: Is CauseFlow secure for production data?
q3.answer: Yes. Agents receive temporary read-only credentials scoped to their data source, valid for 15 minutes. Customer data is never used to train models for other customers, and all actions are recorded in an immutable audit trail.

hero.title: Connect your entire stack
hero.subtitle: CauseFlow integrates with your existing tools — monitoring, code, databases, and customer support. Each one mapped to a specialized AI agent.

catalog.title: Integration Catalog
catalog.andMoreDescription: We are constantly adding new integrations. Contact us if you need a specific tool.

security.title: Integration security, inherited by design
security.description: Dashboard integrations are handled by a SOC 2 Type II–certified infrastructure partner. Every OAuth flow, credential vault, and audit trail inherits their compliance posture from day one — so CauseFlow never touches raw secrets and every tool stays scoped, encrypted, and auditable.
security.soc2Title: SOC 2 Type II from day one
security.soc2Description: Our integration layer is provided by a SOC 2 Type II–certified partner. All OAuth credentials, tokens, and refresh flows live inside their audited vault — covered by their attestation from the moment you connect a tool.
security.iso27001Description: Credentials are encrypted with per-tenant KMS keys inside the partner vault. Secrets never land in CauseFlow application logs, CI artifacts, or error reports.
security.oauthDescription: Every integration uses OAuth 2.0 with the minimum scope required. Short-lived tokens are rotated automatically; no long-lived API keys sit on our servers.
security.encryptionDescription: All integration credentials grant read-only access during investigations. Any write action — a PR, a runbook execution, a remediation — requires explicit human approval.
security.readOnlyDescription: Every call the agent makes through an integration is logged: who, when, which endpoint, which scope. The trail lives in your workspace and is exportable for audits.
security.tenantIsolationDescription: Credentials, tokens, and cached responses are partitioned per tenant at every layer. Cross-tenant access is architecturally impossible — not just policy-forbidden.

setupMinutes.title: Setup in minutes
setupMinutes.description: OAuth or API key —no complex setup, no agent to install. Average setup: 3 minutes per integration.

When a customer reports a problem, CauseFlow investigates the technical root cause and the business impact together — generating both a fix and a customer-facing explanation.

lgpd.name: LGPD
lgpd.details: KMS per-tenant + TTL + right-to-delete. Data subject requests fulfilled in 15 days. Breach notification in 72h.

gdpr.name: GDPR
gdpr.details: Same mechanisms cover GDPR. Standard Contractual Clauses for cross-border transfers.

soc2Type2.name: SOC 2 Type II
soc2Type2.details: Full audit cycle with independent assessor. Automated compliance platform in use.

iso27001.name: ISO 27001
iso27001.details: Information security management system certification planned.

architecture.title: Security Architecture
architecture.waf: Client (HTTPS) -> Web Application Firewall -> Load Balancer -> ECS Tasks (private subnets)
architecture.nat: ECS Tasks -> Network Gateway -> External APIs (Slack, GitHub, etc.)
architecture.credentials: Credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation
architecture.database: Managed Database/NoSQL Database in private subnet with no internet exposure
architecture.logs: All logs centralized in Monitoring & Alerting + Immutable Object Storage
architecture.encryption: Data at rest: AES-256 per-tenant encryption keys. Data in transit: TLS 1.3

isolation.title: Data Isolation (Multi-tenancy)
isolation.application: Tenant ID required in every query, log and record. LLM calls containing data from exactly one tenant.
isolation.database: Database with Row Level Security (RLS) by tenant_id.
isolation.vectorDb: Partitioned vector storage with hard isolation per tenant. Designed to scale to millions of tenants.
isolation.infrastructure: Each investigation runs in ephemeral container with restricted permissions. Container destroyed upon completion.
isolation.piiGateway: Open-source PII detection engine detects and anonymizes emails, phones, SSNs, cards. Reversible anonymization.
isolation.encryption: Data at rest: AES-256 per-tenant encryption keys. Data in transit: TLS 1.3.

bedrock.title: Why AWS Bedrock: The Strongest Privacy Guarantees for AI
bedrock.noTraining: AWS does not use customer data to train models
bedrock.zeroAccess: Model providers have zero access to prompts/completions

integrationSecurity.title: Integration Security
integrationSecurity.subtitle: Every connection CauseFlow makes to your tools is secured at every layer — from credential storage to data transmission.

soc2.title: SOC 2 Certified Integration Infrastructure
soc2.description: Our integration infrastructure is SOC 2 certified — independently audited to confirm that your credentials and access tokens are managed with enterprise-grade security controls.

iso27001.title: ISO 27001:2022 Certified
iso27001.description: ISO 27001:2022 certification for our integration layer confirms that information security management processes meet the latest international standard.

oauth2.title: OAuth 2.0 & Encrypted Credentials
oauth2.description: Integrations use OAuth 2.0 where available. API keys are encrypted at rest with per-tenant KMS keys and never stored in plaintext or exposed in logs.

encryptedCredentials.title: AES-256 Encryption at Rest
encryptedCredentials.description: All integration credentials are encrypted with AES-256 using per-tenant encryption keys. Rotating keys are managed via AWS KMS with automatic rotation.

readOnly.title: Read-Only Access by Default
readOnly.description: CauseFlow requests only read permissions when connecting to your tools. Write operations — such as opening a fix PR — require explicit human approval before execution.

tenantIsolation.title: Tenant Isolation
tenantIsolation.description: Every integration credential and access token is cryptographically isolated per customer account. It is architecturally impossible for one customer's credentials to be accessed by another.

cta.title: Enterprise Security for Teams That Move Fast

hero.title: AI that investigates production incidents,
hero.titleEm: so your engineers don't have to.
hero.metaDescription: CauseFlow exists to close the gap between shipping fast and recovering fast. An AI investigator that runs inside your infrastructure.
hero.metaTitle: About CauseFlow — Built by engineers who lived the 3 AM

mission.body: Make production reliability accessible to every engineering team — not just the ones with six-figure observability budgets. We believe the next decade of software will be defined by how fast teams ship AI-generated code, and how fast they can recover when that code breaks. CauseFlow exists to close that gap.

story.title: Why is a machine still waiting
story.titleEm: on a human to correlate logs?
paragraphs: I spent 13 years in engineering, the last years as an Engineering Manager leading SRE, Security, and GenAI teams at Pagaleve, a Brazilian fintech. Production incidents weren't an occasional fire — they were a weekly ritual. System down. War call on Slack. Seven engineers scrolling through CloudWatch, GitHub commits, and stale dashboards, trying to piece together what broke. Hours lost. Post-mortem written in a rush the next morning.
One question kept coming back: why is a machine still waiting on a human to correlate logs?

The tools that existed were built for U.S. enterprises with compliance teams, six-figure budgets, and English-speaking engineers. For most teams in Brazil and Latin America, they were either unaffordable, inaccessible, or — and this is the part nobody talks about — impossible to adopt because they required shipping sensitive production data to a vendor's cloud. Finance, healthcare, and regulated industries just couldn't say yes.

So we built CauseFlow. An AI agent that investigates incidents the way your best on-call engineer would — except it runs inside your infrastructure, masks sensitive data before anything leaves your environment, and delivers a root-cause timeline in minutes. Not a chatbot. Not a summarizer. An investigator.

cards.title: Privacy by architecture, not by policy
cards.description: A Docker agent runs inside your infrastructure and masks PII, secrets, and sensitive debug data before transmission. GDPR Article 25 and LGPD-aligned by design — not by a pinky promise.

cards.title: Usage-based pricing
cards.description: No seat licenses. No annual contracts. You pay for what you use, which means we only win when you win.

cards.title: Built by engineers who lived the 3 AM
cards.description: Every product decision is pattern-matched against real incident response — not a PM's whiteboard.

founder.name: Vinicius Carvalho

q1.question: What counts as an investigation?
q1.answer: Every time CauseFlow receives a problem and runs a complete investigation (connecting to sources, analyzing data, generating report) counts as 1 investigation.

q2.question: Can I change plans?
q2.answer: Yes, at any time. Upgrade is immediate, downgrade on next cycle.

q3.question: Is there a free trial?
q3.answer: The open-source build is self-hosted — clone the repo and run the bundled Docker Compose stack locally. See the docs for the quickstart.

q4.question: Is there an annual discount?
q4.answer: Yes. Annual billing saves 15% — prices shown with the annual toggle reflect the discounted rate.

q5.question: What if I exceed the limit?
q5.answer: The self-hosted OSS build does not enforce commercial credit quotas or paid overage. Scale investigations on your own infrastructure.

q6.question: How does it work for Brazilian companies?
q6.answer: Self-host CauseFlow inside your environment. Data stays on your stack — LGPD compliant by architecture, not by vendor policy.

overage.title: Need more? Add as you go.
overage.subtitle: When you need additional investigations or events beyond your plan, purchase extra quota directly from the Dashboard — auto-charge or quota packs, your choice.

hero.title: Migrating from Opsgenie?
hero.subtitle: Atlassian is sunsetting Opsgenie in April 2027. Don't just replace your alerting tool — upgrade to AI-powered incident investigation.

whatsHappening.title: What's Happening
whatsHappening.body: Atlassian announced End of Sale for Opsgenie on June 4, 2025 and End of Support on April 5, 2027. After that date, access will be shut off, unmigrated data will be deleted, and APIs will stop working. The recommended migration path is Jira Service Management — but community feedback has been overwhelmingly negative, with engineers describing JSM as "a ticketing system with alerting layered on top." The migration tool only supports Cloud — Data Center customers have no automated path at all.

card1.title: Beyond Alerting
card1.body: Opsgenie routes alerts. CauseFlow investigates them. Connect your tools and get AI-powered root cause analysis in minutes — not just a notification that something is wrong.

card2.title: Usage-Based Pricing
card2.body: No per-seat pricing. No annual contracts. Pay only for what you use — starting at $99/month for 15 investigations. Scale as you need.

card3.title: Setup in Under 10 Minutes
card3.body: Connect via OAuth or AWS IAM Role. No migration tool needed, no infrastructure changes. CauseFlow reads from your existing tools — Slack, GitHub, Jira, CloudWatch, and more.

finalCta.title: Don't Wait Until April 2027
finalCta.subtitle: Be among the first to investigate incidents with AI. Read the docs and start solving incidents faster.

meta.title: Migrating from Opsgenie

connected.title: Connected Mode
connected.description: Direct read-only access to your systems via AWS IAM Role or OAuth2. Fastest setup, real-time analysis.
features: Read-only access — no write permissions required
Real-time analysis across all connected tools
Full audit trail of every access

privacyPreserving.title: Privacy-Preserving Mode
privacyPreserving.description: A lightweight Docker agent runs inside your infrastructure — on-premise or private cloud. It processes, masks, and anonymizes all sensitive data (PII, API keys, debug logs) before anything leaves your environment. This is edge-based data minimization aligned with GDPR Article 25 and LGPD — a Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET) that lets your team use AI-powered investigation without exposing sensitive production data.
features: Raw data never leaves your environment
GDPR Article 25 & LGPD compliant data minimization
Reversible mapping stays in your infrastructure

product.title: How CauseFlow Connects to Your Systems
product.subtitle: Choose full access for maximum speed, or privacy-preserving mode for maximum data protection. Both deliver the same root cause.

security.title: Privacy-Preserving Architecture: Your Data Never Leaves
security.description: CauseFlow is the only incident investigation platform that deploys a Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET) directly in your infrastructure. A Docker agent processes, masks, and anonymizes sensitive data at the edge — hostnames, IPs, user IDs, API keys, service names — before any transmission. Your raw data never leaves your security perimeter. The AI cloud receives only what it needs for root cause analysis, with sensitive identifiers replaced by opaque tokens.
guarantees: Reversible mapping stays in your infrastructure

compare.calloutTitle: The Only Platform with Privacy-Preserving Architecture
compare.calloutDescription: Investigate incidents with edge-based data minimization — your raw data never leaves your infrastructure. A Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET) included free on every plan. No other incident investigation platform offers this level of data protection.

stalePricing.metaTitle: Stale Pricing Page — CauseFlow Case Study
stalePricing.metaDescription: How CauseFlow identified a CDN caching issue serving stale pricing data 48 hours before an enterprise sales demo.

hero.headline: Pricing page froze
hero.lead: A missing tag mapping and a silent TypeError combined to block every revalidation job — leaving SimUser AI's pricing page stuck on old data right before a 15% price repositioning went live.

symptom.heading: The Symptom
symptom.body: On April 16, 2026, Felipe Aguiar (Head of Marketing, SimUser AI) pushed a 15% price update across all plans via Contentful at 11:20 UTC. At 11:45 UTC a lead confirmed the /pricing page still showed the old numbers. The marketing team had already shared the updated pricing in an email blast — visitors clicking through saw stale data.

diagram.heading: Where the Pipeline Broke
diagram.subheading: Two overlapping bugs blocked every revalidation attempt. Hover each X to see the failing code.

routesSection.heading: Systemic Blast Radius
routesSection.body: The TypeError in the Subscriber handler affected every route that had a queued revalidation job — not just /pricing. Any page touched by a DynamoDB Streams event during this window was silently stuck.

timeline.heading: Symptom → Root Cause
timeline.step1Inference: Lead reports old plan prices; CMS publish was 25 min ago.
timeline.step2Inference: TypeError on NewImage blocks all revalidation jobs — any DELETE event crashes the handler.
timeline.step3Inference: Tag 'pricing-page' has no paths registered in the revalidation table; pathCount: 0.
timeline.step4Inference: Missing tag map + unguarded NewImage access = no revalidation ever reaches Next.js.

fix.heading: Root Cause & Fix
fix.bug1Body: The CMS webhook sends the tag pricing-page, but no entry in the DynamoDB table maps that tag to the /pricing path. The Seeder logs a warn and exits without queuing any invalidation.
fix.bug2Body: The RevalidationEventsSubscriber reads event.Records[i].dynamodb.NewImage unconditionally. DynamoDB Streams DELETE events omit NewImage, causing a TypeError that puts every affected job into an unrecoverable retries: 3 / status: pending state.
fix.beforeLabel: Before — handler crashes on DELETE events
fix.tagNote: Additionally: register the tag pricing-page → /pricing in the revalidation table and audit all other CMS tags to ensure they map to the correct Next.js paths.

outcome.heading: Outcome
outcome.stat: Without CauseFlow: ~3 hrs of cross-team debugging across CloudWatch, DynamoDB console, and Lambda logs — with the reputational cost of stale pricing visible to inbound leads during a live price repositioning.
outcome.withCauseflow: With CauseFlow: root cause surfaced in 25 minutes, fix committed before any further leads landed on the page.

brokenImages.metaTitle: Broken Images During Demo — CauseFlow Case Study
brokenImages.metaDescription: How CauseFlow traced broken product screenshots to a Lambda cold-start timeout that blew the image optimizer budget mid-demo.

hero.headline: Eight minutes of broken images,
hero.headlineEm: and every demo screenshot was a 504.
hero.lead: Rodrigo Lima was mid-demo with a prospect when every .webp on the page returned a broken icon. The CDN looked healthy. CauseFlow found the real ceiling: a Lambda cold start that consumed 3,098 ms of a 5,000 ms budget — leaving zero time for the image fetches.

budgetSection.title: The Lambda Budget Clock
budgetSection.lead: Every image optimization request has a 5,000 ms budget. A cold start ate 3,098 ms of it before a single pixel was fetched.

coldStartSection.title: Cold-Start Math
coldStartSection.lead: Three image fetches launched concurrently the moment the Lambda initialised — all three ran straight into the budget wall.
coldStartSection.caption: INIT_DURATION 3098 ms + fetch 2500 ms ≈ 5598 ms > 5000 ms budget → Timeout

cdnSection.title: CloudFront Hypothesis — Dismissed
cdnSection.lead: The team's first instinct was CDN. CloudFront metrics showed no anomalies — cache hit rate normal, no origin errors reported. The problem was upstream of the CDN entirely.
cardLines: OriginLatency: n/a   ← no origin errors logged
Conclusion: problem is upstream of CDN.

logDiffSection.title: Self-Healing Log Diff
logDiffSection.lead: Once a warm Lambda instance handled subsequent requests, the INIT_START overhead vanished — images served in 312 ms. Classic cold-start self-resolution.

fixSection.title: Three Paths Forward
fixSection.lead: CauseFlow surfaces options. Your team decides.
fixSection.option1Tradeoff: Keeps one Lambda instance always warm. Eliminates cold starts during business hours. Cost: ~$15–20/month per warm instance.
fixSection.option2Tradeoff: Separate Lambda per image type reduces init payload and cold-start duration. More infrastructure to maintain.
fixSection.option3Tradeoff: Bumps the Lambda timeout to 10 s — buys breathing room on cold starts. Does not prevent them. Latency degrades for end users.

rootCauseSection.title: Root Cause
rootCauseSection.body: The WebsiteImageOptimizerFunction cold-started at 3,098 ms — 62% of the 5,000 ms total budget — leaving only 1,902 ms for three concurrent .webp fetches that each needed ~2,500 ms. All three timed out, CloudFront received 504, and the browser rendered broken images for the ~8 minutes it took for a warm instance to become available.

ctaSection.description: Connect your stack in minutes and investigate real incidents on your infra. No contract, no card.

cascading500.metaDescription: How CauseFlow traced cascading 500 errors to a CMS outage with zero cache fallback — diagnosed in under 5 minutes during a LinkedIn traffic spike.

hero.headline: Ten minutes of 500s during a LinkedIn spike —
hero.headlineEm: with zero cache to fall back on
hero.lead: A LinkedIn post drove a wave of new visitors to SimUser AI's site. The CMS buckled under load and returned 503s. With no cache layer and no fallback, every request hit a live 500 — for ten straight minutes.

arch.sectionLead: Every page request fetched content directly from the CMS at runtime. No cache sat between the Lambda function and the CMS — making the CMS a single point of failure.

chart.sectionLead: Request volume and error count rose in lockstep. The CMS couldn't absorb the spike — each new visitor hit a 500 instead of a page.
chart.title: Traffic and error rate during the LinkedIn spike

cache.sectionTitle: The Cache That Was Never Warm
cache.sectionLead: A DynamoDB scan confirmed the root cause: 80 cache records, every one with revalidatedAt: 1 — the Unix epoch sentinel for "never revalidated since deploy".
cache.title: DynamoDB · WebsiteRevalidationTable
cache.findingDescription: All 80 cache entries for the affected page show revalidatedAt: 1 — the cache was set at deploy time and never warmed. When the CMS went down, there was nothing to serve.

beforeAfter.sectionLead: The fix was structural — migrate from SSR-on-every-request to ISR with a 5-minute revalidation window and a try/catch fallback to stale cache.
beforeAfter.beforeSubtitle: SSR — fetch on every request, no cache
beforeAfter.afterSubtitle: ISR + fallback — stale cache beats a 500

impact.statCaption: Based on average conversion rate and traffic volume during the 10-minute window. Each visitor who hit a 500 on the Get Started page was a potential trial signup.
impact.statNote: Estimate: 400 visitors × 10% signup rate × 10 min outage

rootCause.sectionLead: The page used pure SSR with a synchronous CMS fetch on every request. The fix: migrate to ISR with revalidate: 300 and a try/catch fallback that serves stale content instead of throwing.
rootCause.afterCodeTitle: After — ISR + fallback: CMS failure → stale cache

cta.headlineP1: Stop hunting for the root cause
cta.description: CauseFlow correlates DynamoDB records, Lambda logs, and CloudWatch metrics automatically — no manual log triage, no post-mortem archaeology.
